title: Development Arrested

jokes.text: So why is this man crying? Because now he'll have to stay with these people for a long, long time.
jokes.setup: Michael is being honored by his family for keeping them together — a triumphant moment.
jokes.punchline: The narrator reframes his tears as horror at being stuck with these awful people, undercutting the triumph.
jokes.explanation: The narrator's dry subversion of what should be a heartwarming moment is quintessential AD. The economy is superb — two sentences invert the entire emotional framing. The joke earns its score because it's also thematically true about the whole series: Michael's 'success' is a trap. High rewatch value because the finale makes this even more pointed.

jokes.text: It was the monster called lust, to which he'd recently succumbed.
jokes.setup: George Michael is in Michael's bed, described as scared of a monster.
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals the 'monster' is his own romantic feelings for his cousin Maeby — a dramatically overwrought framing for an adolescent crush.
jokes.explanation: The narrator's mock-Gothic framing of a teenage crush as a 'monster called lust' is perfectly calibrated deadpan. It works on multiple levels: absurd melodrama, the actual dark comedy of cousin-attraction, and the narrator's sardonic voice. Strong craft, loses points only because it's slightly one-note.

jokes.text: Although he'd only gotten to second base, he'd gone in head first, like Pete Rose.
jokes.setup: George Michael and Maeby's romantic entanglement is described using baseball metaphors.
jokes.punchline: Pete Rose is famous for headfirst slides AND for being banned from baseball for gambling — a double-layered gag implying both the physical act and a reckless disregard for rules.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional layering. The baseball metaphor for romantic bases is standard, but invoking Pete Rose's headfirst slide PLUS his rule-breaking persona to describe cousin-incest is genuinely clever and original. The double meaning rewards attentive viewers. This is precisely the kind of dense, multi-layered joke AD is celebrated for. Earns the 8.0 craft rating.

jokes.text: And there you are, Maeby, coming out of your mother's third base.
jokes.setup: Tobias is showing 'rough pictures' of Maeby's birth to prove she is Lindsay's daughter; the baseball metaphor from the narrator continues.
jokes.punchline: Tobias calls the birth canal 'third base' — an unintentionally sexual/crude malapropism from Tobias that also continues the baseball thread.
jokes.explanation: This is a callback that pays off the baseball metaphor with Tobias's signature oblivious double-entendre. Only Tobias could describe a birth photo this way — perfect character integration. The fact that it continues the narrator's baseball conceit while Tobias unknowingly makes it sexual is layered comedy writing. The 'rough pictures' setup is also its own joke (birth photos ARE rough). One of the episode's strongest moments.

jokes.text: You're not sleeping in here now. / What, so we can make out every night? / Does that not work for your schedule?
jokes.setup: After confirming they're related, Maeby says George Michael can't sleep in her room.
jokes.punchline: George Michael's flustered response implies he's actually suggesting the nightly make-out sessions continue — then doubles down when called out.
jokes.explanation: George Michael's awkward, foot-in-mouth escalation is funny because he can't stop himself from making it worse. The cringe compounds naturally. Good character work but not exceptional.

jokes.text: What have I always said is the most important thing? / Family. / I was gonna say breakfast, but...
jokes.setup: Michael asks rhetorically what the most important thing is, expecting 'family.'
jokes.punchline: George Michael correctly guesses 'family' but reveals he was about to say 'breakfast' — undermining the sentimental moment with mundane honesty.
jokes.explanation: A tight, well-structured subversion. The setup is a classic sitcom sentimental beat; the punchline deflates it with perfect comic timing and George Michael's characteristically earnest obliviousness. Economy is excellent — the 'but...' trailing off is the right move. High quotability.

jokes.text: Risky! Risky! Risky! — the family chanting at the party brunch
jokes.setup: The Bluth stock was bumped from 'don't buy' to 'risky' on Mad Money, which the family celebrated with glee.
jokes.punchline: The family repeats 'Risky! Risky! Risky!' as a cheer at the brunch, treating a mediocre stock rating as a triumphant achievement.
jokes.explanation: The joke is the Bluths' complete lack of standards — celebrating a 'risky' rating like a championship. The callback structure works well and the family's unified obliviousness is on-brand, but it's more of a solid running gag payoff than a standout moment.

jokes.text: Look at that, Buster. You would have lost a hand anyway.
jokes.setup: Buster lost his hand to a seal attack. Now they're near the ocean again.
jokes.punchline: Lucille casually points out that Buster would have lost his hand regardless — delivered as dark comfort, implying some prior plan or fate involving his hand.
jokes.explanation: Perfectly dark Lucille line. The casual cruelty ('you would have lost it anyway') is both a running gag callback and a perfect encapsulation of Lucille's monstrous maternal indifference. Character integration is near-perfect — this is uniquely a Lucille-to-Buster joke. High rewatch bonus for long-time viewers.

jokes.text: Oh, I'm not comfortable near the ocean since the seal attack. The ocean is my second biggest fear.
jokes.setup: Lucille announces the party will be on a boat.
jokes.punchline: Buster objects due to the seal attack, but then clarifies the ocean is only his SECOND biggest fear — implying there's something even scarier than the ocean/seal that attacked him.
jokes.explanation: Classic AD foreshadowing joke. The 'second biggest fear' is funny on its own (what could be worse than the seal that ate your hand?), and it pays off beautifully later when Buster's first fear — actual seals — is revealed. Rewatch bonus is high because the payoff is earned.

jokes.text: Good, don't come. I need to be rubbing elbows, not working your fork.
jokes.setup: Buster expresses discomfort at coming to the boat party.
jokes.punchline: Lucille dismisses him with utter cruelty — she doesn't want him there anyway, framing his absence as a social convenience for her.
jokes.explanation: Another perfect Lucille cruelty moment. 'Rubbing elbows, not working your fork' is a genuinely sharp turn of phrase that encapsulates her social climbing and her contempt for Buster simultaneously. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: No magic, Gob. Where is Gob? Oh, who knows? I don't even know where he lives. / He doesn't live at Michael's? / I've always pictured him in a lighthouse.
jokes.setup: The family casually wonders where Gob lives.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay has just always mentally pictured Gob in a lighthouse — a completely random, specific, and perfect image that says everything about how the family thinks of Gob.
jokes.explanation: The 'lighthouse' line is perfectly absurdist — specific enough to be funny on its own, and then it pays off when Gob IS living on a boat (not far off!). The joke works on three levels: absurdist image, character indifference, and structural foreshadowing. One of the episode's most quotable moments. The payoff at 06:18 ('Lighthouse wasn't far off') elevates it further.

jokes.text: Stan Sitwell's always had a wild hair to buy this business. It's the only hair he's got. / He's an alpaca. / He has alopecia.
jokes.setup: Michael uses the idiom 'wild hair' about Sitwell; Lindsay mishears/misunderstands 'alopecia' as 'alpaca.'
jokes.punchline: The triple escalation: Michael's idiom → Lindsay's 'alpaca' insult → Lucille's correction 'alopecia' — Lindsay has confused a hair-loss condition with a wool-producing animal.
jokes.explanation: A masterfully layered joke. The idiom 'wild hair' sets up a wordplay chain: the only hair he has → Lindsay's malapropism 'alpaca' (genuinely sounds like alopecia AND is a funny animal) → the correction. It's character-perfect for Lindsay (vain, not bright) and for Lucille (correcting without actually being kind). The economy is tight — three beats, maximum comedy. Near the top tier of this episode.

jokes.text: Two million! Two million! — the family's reaction to the potential payout
jokes.setup: Michael mentions selling would yield 'like, what, two million dollars each.'
jokes.punchline: The family erupts in excitement — despite having been wealthy their whole lives, they treat two million as an astronomical sum, revealing how thoroughly they've lost all perspective.
jokes.explanation: Solid group character comedy — the Bluths' complete financial irresponsibility made vivid. The joke is functional and fits the pattern established throughout the series, but isn't particularly inventive.

jokes.text: That sounds like a lot more than it actually is. You've already spent $1,200 on eggs, and that would be it forever.
jokes.setup: The family celebrates two million dollars.
jokes.punchline: Michael undercuts with the specific '$1,200 on eggs' — a hilariously precise example of Bluth wastefulness that makes two million seem paltry.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of '$1,200 on eggs' is the joke's engine. It's not a round number, which makes it feel researched and real. Michael's deadpan delivery of this absurd data point captures both his exhaustion and the Bluths' genuine insanity. Strong economy.

jokes.text: Keep in mind, we are building something for our kids... it's also for George Michael and Maeby's kids too. / We can't have kids! / It's not even an option really.
jokes.setup: Michael makes a sentimental speech about the family's future and their children's children.
jokes.punchline: George Michael and Maeby both immediately and forcefully shut down any possibility of having children together — to the confused room — because they've been making out and she's (apparently) his cousin.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully timed cringe beat. Michael's innocent remark lands as an accusation in context, and the two cousins' panicked denial reveals their guilt without Michael understanding why. The 'It's not even an option really' is the kind of overly emphatic denial that makes the joke land. Good structural irony.

jokes.text: I, for one, will not go back to wondering whether there's going to be enough food on the table. / Might wanna start worrying about the table.
jokes.setup: Lucille makes a dramatic statement about food insecurity (completely absurd given the family's wealth).
jokes.punchline: Tobias quietly suggests the actual table — implying it's been sold or is inadequate — deflating Lucille's grandiosity with a practical observation.
jokes.explanation: A sharp one-liner with perfect economy. Lucille's faux-martyrdom is punctured by Tobias's single word pivot. The reversal is fast and clean — she worries about the food, he points out there's no table. Good structure, excellent economy.

jokes.text: You know, being twins, our birthdays are pretty close to one another.
jokes.setup: Lindsay mentions she'll be 40 in three years; Michael responds about their shared birthday as twins.
jokes.punchline: Michael states the obvious (twins have 'pretty close' birthdays) with complete sincerity — an absurd understatement that also reveals he's been so focused on the company he's stopped thinking straight.
jokes.explanation: A subtle, dry joke that rewards attentive viewers. Michael understating 'pretty close' for 'identical' is the joke — the absurdity of a twin saying this. Doesn't quite reach top tier because it requires a beat to land but works well in context.

jokes.text: A 40-year-old woman has as much chance of getting married as she does of getting attacked by a shark. / Oh, I'm sorry, I thought you were going to say seal. / Or a seal. / Oh, God!
jokes.setup: Michael uses the old 'attacked by a shark' statistic as a dark joke about Lindsay's marriage prospects.
jokes.punchline: Buster, traumatized by his seal attack, assumed the threatening animal would be a seal. Then Michael casually adds 'Or a seal,' and Lindsay reacts in horror — now her marriage odds are tied to seal attacks.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best joke and one of the great AD moments. Three layers: (1) the feminist statistic reframed as a dark joke, (2) Buster's trauma inserting 'seal' in a moment about marriage odds, (3) Michael casually validating it ('Or a seal'), and (4) Lindsay's horrified reaction. Every character beat is perfect — Michael's oblivious cruelty, Buster's PTSD interruption, the callback payoff. The 'Or a seal' addition is the stroke of genius: Michael doesn't correct Buster, he just includes it. Comparable to the anchor's 8.0 example in sharpness, and surpasses it in layering. Deserves the 8.8 craft rating.

jokes.text: Girls just grow faster than boys. Dinner's ready. We're having Lindsay chops. / What? / I want her to be prepared in case some bully at school is as clever as I am.
jokes.setup: Lucille is defending Lindsay's weight/appearance with a hollow platitude.
jokes.punchline: Then we hear Lucille calling dinner 'Lindsay chops' — naming the food after her daughter — and justifying it as toughening Lindsay up for bullies, while simultaneously admitting she's the one bullying her.
jokes.explanation: A devastating character joke. 'Lindsay chops' is monstrous, and Lucille's self-aware justification — 'in case some bully at school is as clever as I am' — is both the confession and the excuse simultaneously. The 'No bully ever would be' response makes it darker. This is uniquely a Lucille joke: she IS the bully, she knows it, and she frames it as love. Near-perfect character integration score justified.

jokes.text: No bully ever would be.
jokes.setup: Lucille says she's preparing Lindsay for bullies as clever as herself.
jokes.punchline: Michael flatly confirms no bully would ever be that cruel — which is simultaneously a compliment to Lucille's wit and a condemnation of her parenting.
jokes.explanation: A perfect button on the 'Lindsay chops' joke. Three words that say everything about Lucille. The economy is nearly perfect — no waste, maximum implication. Loses points because it functions as a tag to joke #19 rather than standing alone.

jokes.text: Oh! Well, your mom's not gonna allow for that. She'll do whatever it takes to get her way.
jokes.setup: Michael says there will be plenty of money from an honestly run business.
jokes.punchline: George Sr. immediately dismisses this — Lucille won't allow honesty. The matter-of-fact delivery treats dishonesty as a simple corporate constraint.
jokes.explanation: Functional joke — George Sr.'s resigned corruption is on-brand and gets the point across, but it's not inventive. The payoff (Lucille deported the banana stand's original inventor) is the real punchline; this is setup.

jokes.text: The Frozen Banana itself was the brainchild of a Korean immigrant who threatened to sue them. Your mother said she'd take care of it. The guy was deported to Korea the next day.
jokes.setup: George Sr. reveals that not even the banana stand was the Bluths' idea.
jokes.punchline: Lucille's 'taking care of it' meant getting the inventor deported — a shockingly dark escalation delivered with total casualness.
jokes.explanation: The banana stand is the show's most iconic running gag, and this retroactive origin story is a brilliant subversion. Lucille had someone deported rather than pay royalties — played completely straight by the narrator. The economy of 'the next day' as the reveal timing is perfect. High rewatch bonus for series fans who know the banana stand's significance.

jokes.text: Where his business never recovered.
jokes.setup: The Korean inventor was deported to Korea after Lucille had him removed.
jokes.punchline: The narrator adds the dry coda that his business 'never recovered' — as if the real tragedy is the business failure, not the deportation.
jokes.explanation: Perfect narrator deadpan — the economy is exceptional (four words, tremendous dark implication). The joke works because it treats deportation as a business setback rather than a human injustice, which is pitch-perfectly in keeping with the show's satirical voice.

jokes.text: She's the queen, Michael. I was her king, and now it's you.
jokes.setup: George Sr. explains that Lucille has always been the mastermind behind all the crimes.
jokes.punchline: He frames Michael as the new 'king' — but in context, the king is clearly the lesser power, serving the queen's agenda. George Sr. is warning Michael he's about to become the next patsy.
jokes.explanation: More thematic than comedic, but it lands as dark irony. The chess metaphor works because in chess, the king is nominally powerful but actually the most vulnerable piece. Solid craft, but the joke is more of a dramatic beat with comedic undertones.

jokes.text: Michael went to cancel the reservation on the party boat Buster so feared. Gob! When he saw Gob on a party boat of his own.
jokes.setup: Michael goes to cancel a boat reservation.
jokes.punchline: He finds Gob already living on a different party boat — the visual reveal answers Lindsay's question about where Gob lives.
jokes.explanation: The payoff of the lighthouse joke — Gob lives on a boat, which is close enough. The reveal is well-timed and rewards viewers who caught the earlier gag. Structural callback works well.

jokes.text: You might wanna untie these ropes first. / Don't tell me how to flee, Michael.
jokes.setup: Gob is apparently attempting to flee on his boat (still tied to the dock).
jokes.punchline: Gob's defensive 'Don't tell me how to flee' implies he has extensive experience fleeing and considers himself an expert — despite clearly doing it wrong.
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Gob line. 'Don't tell me how to flee' is the perfect encapsulation of Gob's defensive incompetence — he's terrible at the thing he's claiming expertise in, and his indignation makes it funnier. High quotability; the line stands alone without context. One of the episode's most memorable bits of dialogue.

jokes.text: Lighthouse wasn't far off.
jokes.setup: Lindsay said earlier she 'always pictured Gob in a lighthouse.'
jokes.punchline: The narrator notes that living on a boat isn't far from a lighthouse — a quick, dry callback payoff.
jokes.explanation: Masterful economy — four words to close a callback loop. The AD narrator's voice makes throwaway lines like this sing. The joke is better because it doesn't over-explain; it trusts the audience.

jokes.text: Perhaps you can tell me how you can afford a boat. / You mean on the paltry 1,500 a week you give me? / I give you 500 a week. / Yeah, well, somebody's drawing a little one on those checks. No big deal.
jokes.setup: Michael questions how Gob affords a yacht on his allowance.
jokes.punchline: Gob reveals someone has been altering Michael's checks — and then casually admits it's him ('No big deal'), having tripled his own allowance by adding a '1' in front of the amount.
jokes.explanation: An exceptional character joke. Gob's confession is delivered with such breezy casualness ('No big deal') that the audacity becomes the punchline. The specificity of 'drawing a little one on those checks' is perfect — it's the dumbest possible fraud, executed with total confidence. Pure Gob. The escalating reveal structure (1500 → 500 → 'somebody' → him) is textbook AD layering.

jokes.text: You give a man a fish, he'll eat for a night. / If you teach a man to fish... / He'll wanna use your yacht.
jokes.setup: Michael tries to lecture Gob with the proverb 'teach a man to fish.'
jokes.punchline: Gob completes it with 'He'll wanna use your yacht' — turning a proverb about self-sufficiency into a complaint about people wanting his stuff, which is exactly backwards from the proverb's meaning and perfectly on-brand for Gob.
jokes.explanation: The best pure joke in the episode and one of the great Gob lines. The setup is the famous proverb; the punchline utterly inverts its meaning while being perfectly character-consistent. Gob is so self-absorbed that he hears a lesson about giving and turns it into a complaint about people wanting his yacht. The structure is flawless — Michael sets up the proverb, Gob hijacks it in a direction that reveals everything about him. Maximum economy, maximum character integration. Comparable to and arguably exceeding the 8.0 anchor example.

jokes.text: I don't want this thing smelling like fish. I'm burning through my future for it. But you wouldn't understand that because you're a robot.
jokes.setup: Gob explains why he won't fish off his yacht.
jokes.punchline: He calls Michael a 'robot' as the most insulting thing he can think of — Gob's emotional reasoning is so chaotic that a robot is his go-to slur.
jokes.explanation: Solid Gob character work — his bizarre insults always reveal more about him than about the target. 'Robot' as the worst thing he can imagine is funny but not transcendent.

jokes.text: Oh, really? How would it feel to lose the company to a man who has less hair on his !@%$ than I have on my !@%$#.
jokes.setup: Michael is reluctant to give Gob the yacht to keep him from selling shares.
jokes.punchline: The speaker (implied Lucille) makes a crude comparison about Sitwell's alopecia extending to... other body parts, deploying shocking vulgarity in service of a business argument.
jokes.explanation: The shock value of the vulgarity combined with the business context is funny, and it's a callback to the alopecia gag. The censoring ([!@%$]) makes it funnier by implication. Loses points for relying partly on crude shock value rather than pure wit.

jokes.text: It can't be worse than hearing that just was.
jokes.setup: Someone just made a crude remark about body hair.
jokes.punchline: Michael's deadpan response acknowledges the horror of what was just said — his understated revulsion is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Clean reaction beat, perfectly Michael — his suffering is a running joke of the show. Not exceptional, but functional and well-timed.

jokes.text: I couldn't see through the glasses, and I slid into the ladder.
jokes.setup: Tobias appears injured after offscreen physical comedy.
jokes.punchline: His explanation reveals he was wearing the wrong glasses (implying costume/character glasses for one of his roles) and injured himself — typical Tobias obliviousness.
jokes.explanation: Mildly funny — Tobias's accident-prone obliviousness is a character staple, but this particular instance doesn't have strong specificity or surprise. Functional filler.

jokes.text: Me too. / What do you mean, Son? You kissed? / Even a little more than that.
jokes.setup: George Michael confesses he and Maeby 'made out'; Michael responds 'Sorry?'
jokes.punchline: George Michael interprets 'Sorry?' as an expression of empathy ('Me too') rather than a request to repeat himself — accidentally revealing the depth of his feelings before Michael even understands what happened.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed misunderstanding. 'Me too' lands on multiple levels: George Michael sharing in Michael's hypothetical shock, and also confessing his own complicated feelings. The 'Even a little more than that' escalation compounds the cringe. The structure is tight and character-specific.

jokes.text: They were really rough pictures.
jokes.setup: George Michael explains that Tobias showed them birth pictures proving Maeby is Lindsay's biological daughter. He called them 'rough pictures.'
jokes.punchline: George Michael repeats 'They were really rough pictures' — which initially sounds like he means disturbing/crude photos but means they were poorly developed/amateur birth photos. The double meaning suggests something more salacious.
jokes.explanation: The ambiguity of 'rough pictures' is delightful — birth photos ARE rough in multiple senses. The repetition by George Michael suggests he's still processing the trauma of seeing them. Good layered wordplay.

jokes.text: She's a blood relative. / I know. I've seen the blood.
jokes.setup: Michael tells George Michael that Maeby is a blood relative so acting on his feelings is off-limits.
jokes.punchline: George Michael confirms he knows — he's 'seen the blood' — a reference to Tobias's 'rough pictures' of Maeby's birth, which were apparently very graphic birth photos.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional economy and layering. 'I know. I've seen the blood' works as a solemn confirmation AND as a callback to the 'rough pictures' joke (the blood of childbirth) AND as a darkly funny non-sequitur. The line is shocking in the best way — you don't expect it, and then it instantly resolves into its meaning. High quotability; this line lands perfectly out of context too.

jokes.text: How long has this been going on? / I don't know... 53 weeks?
jokes.setup: Michael asks how long the George Michael/Maeby romantic situation has been going on.
jokes.punchline: George Michael's answer '53 weeks' is strangely specific — not 'a year,' not 'since last summer,' but precisely 53 weeks. The number implies he's been counting, and the extra week beyond a year suggests he's been acutely aware of every day.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of '53 weeks' is doing tremendous work. It's funnier than 'a year' because it reveals the degree of George Michael's obsession — he's been counting. It also maps onto the show's runtime (the series is roughly three seasons, this is the finale, so it's a sly meta-nod). Strong character integration and originality.

jokes.text: Whatever happened to that Ann we were both so crazy about? / Yeah, Ann is the girl for us.
jokes.setup: Michael suggests George Michael distract himself from Maeby by reconnecting with Ann.
jokes.punchline: Michael's 'the girl for us' is a weird slip — 'us' implies he also has a stake in the relationship — playing on the series gag where Michael repeatedly advocates for Ann as if she's his girlfriend too.
jokes.explanation: A solid running gag payoff. Michael's possessive 'the girl for us' is the culmination of the season-long joke where Michael seems more invested in George Michael's relationship with Ann than George Michael is. Works well for fans of the gag.

jokes.text: I'm not going to sell my five percent, even though you haven't made an offer. Which would be how much, by the way?
jokes.setup: Lindsay nobly declares she won't sell her stock even without an offer.
jokes.punchline: In the same breath, she asks what the offer would be — entirely undercutting her noble declaration.
jokes.explanation: A tight self-contradiction that is pure Lindsay. The joke is in the lack of any pause between virtue-signaling and price-shopping. The structure is excellent — the setup establishes her high-minded refusal, the punchline collapses it immediately. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Lucille actually gave you less than her natural children. I hope that's not a crack about my hair color, lips, forehead, nose and teeth.
jokes.setup: Sitwell mentions that Lucille gave Lindsay less stock than her 'natural children.'
jokes.punchline: Lindsay interprets this as a physical insult and lists every body part she's had work done on — inadvertently cataloguing her surgeries while responding to a completely different comment.
jokes.explanation: Superb. The joke works on two levels simultaneously: (1) the misunderstanding is genuinely funny, and (2) the list of body parts inadvertently reveals Lindsay's extensive plastic surgery. 'Hair color, lips, forehead, nose and teeth' is perfectly structured — the list escalates from superficial to surgical. Strong character integration; only Lindsay would be so simultaneously vain and oblivious.

jokes.text: Because at least I'm not wearing a rayon sweater set on my face. / It's alpaca actually. Cruelty-free. Really narrows your choices.
jokes.setup: Lindsay insults Sitwell's facial hair situation; he corrects her on the fabric.
jokes.punchline: Sitwell not only corrects the fabric ('alpaca') but adds 'Cruelty-free. Really narrows your choices' — implying that his ethics-based fashion choices are responsible for his limited options, which is darkly funny given that his 'choices' are constrained by alopecia, not ethics.
jokes.explanation: Sitwell's response is layered — he takes the insult in stride and turns it into a self-deprecating/ethical joke. 'Really narrows your choices' is good because it implies the cruelty-free constraint limits him, but it's actually the alopecia that limits him. The callback to alpaca and the show's tracking of the alpaca/alopecia gag adds value.

jokes.text: Wait a minute. You're saying I'm adopted? / I'm sorry. I thought you knew. / Are you kidding me? This is the happiest I've been since the day I got my new nose.
jokes.setup: Sitwell gently reveals Lindsay is adopted.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's reaction is pure joy — not because of freedom from the Bluths, but her first instinct is to compare it to getting a new nose (plastic surgery). The non-sequitur reveals her entire value system.
jokes.explanation: The callback to the nose from the earlier surgery list is satisfying, and Lindsay's emotional register — euphoria about adoption, compared to rhinoplasty — is perfect character comedy. It subverts every expected adoption-reveal reaction while feeling completely true to Lindsay.

jokes.text: You were almost a Sitwell. But 37 years ago, the Bluths adopted you out from under us. So we said good-bye to you, our darling little three-year-old. / Wait, three-year-old? You said it was 37 years ago.
jokes.setup: Sitwell tells the adoption story, saying they gave up Lindsay 37 years ago when she was three.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay catches the math — 37 years ago minus 3 years old means she's actually 40, not the age she claims. Her moment of self-discovery immediately becomes a vanity crisis.
jokes.explanation: The arithmetic joke is well-constructed — Sitwell slips up mid-sentimental-story, and Lindsay is sharp enough to catch only THIS particular detail (her age), not the broader implications. The structure is excellent: emotional revelation interrupted by vanity math. Character integration is near-perfect — Lindsay ignores the adoption bombshell and focuses on the age reveal.

jokes.text: Oh, yeah, right. Hey, we should do something for your 40th next week.
jokes.setup: Sitwell is caught in his math error about Lindsay's age during an emotional adoption reveal.
jokes.punchline: He casually pivots to birthday planning — the most tone-deaf possible response — without acknowledging the deeper emotional weight of the moment.
jokes.explanation: The pivot to birthday planning is a perfect anticlimax — Sitwell treats his own slip about her secret age as a party planning opportunity. Clean, dry, well-timed.

jokes.text: !@%$, let me !@%$#%!@$%#@! Get your !@%$#%!@$%#@! Be gentle, she's almost 40!
jokes.setup: Lindsay reacts to learning she's actually 40.
jokes.punchline: The narrator's 'Be gentle, she's almost 40!' is the perfect button — the censored violence of Lindsay's outburst is undercut by the patronizing age-related concern.
jokes.explanation: The censored chaos is funny, but the real punchline is the narrator's 'Be gentle, she's almost 40' — treating her as a delicate antique while she's clearly in a rage. The narrator's deadpan in this moment is perfectly timed.

jokes.text: Fire you? Are you kidding? I've already gotten 10 requests for the TV rights to your story.
jokes.setup: Maeby returns to the studio expecting to be fired for lying about her age.
jokes.punchline: Instead, her fraud has made her MORE valuable — her real story is more compelling than anything she green-lit as a producer.
jokes.explanation: Good irony — Maeby's fraud is its own story. The meta-angle (TV show about the Bluths being pitched to executives in the AD universe) will pay off in the finale's epilogue. Solid structural joke.

jokes.text: This is the biggest thing to happen since Uniprod hired that wee-brain to run drama development.
jokes.setup: The executive compares Maeby's story's buzz to another major event in their industry.
jokes.punchline: The 'biggest thing' in their studio's recent history was hiring an incompetent — which itself is a dig at Hollywood development executives and an implied self-burn.
jokes.explanation: A decent jab at Hollywood that fits the show's satire, but it's a little too insider and the character isn't developed enough for the line to have full impact. Functional.

jokes.text: Are you drunk? / A little. We need to talk. / I mean, can you imagine anything more inappropriate? / Hey, I guess you can.
jokes.setup: Michael is upset about George Michael and Maeby's romantic situation. Lindsay arrives drunk to talk.
jokes.punchline: Michael asks if Lindsay can imagine anything more inappropriate — then catches himself, because Lindsay (drunk and now apparently not his sister) is about to proposition him. 'Hey, I guess you can.'
jokes.explanation: Elegant structural irony. Michael walks right into his own hypocrisy — complaining about cousin incest while his (now apparently non-sister) sister is about to hit on him. The 'Hey, I guess you can' is the perfect self-correction delivered with resigned horror. The parallel structure to the George Michael/Maeby storyline is intentional and well-executed.

jokes.text: I'm not your sister. / What? / I'm adopted. And I'm three years older.
jokes.setup: Lindsay is making a move on Michael.
jokes.punchline: Her attempt to make herself MORE appealing involves revealing she's adopted AND older than him — two pieces of information that are meant to be seductive but land as deeply alarming.
jokes.explanation: Lindsay deploying adoption news as a seduction tactic is the perfect cringe escalation. The specificity of 'And I'm three years older' (why would that help?) makes it funnier. The whole sequence is a masterclass in compounding discomfort.

jokes.text: I know you've always found me attractive. You've been telling me that for the last 40 years.
jokes.setup: Lindsay is trying to seduce Michael.
jokes.punchline: She interprets 40 years of Michael's obligatory 'you're beautiful' encouragements (given repeatedly to build her confidence) as genuine attraction — completely misconstruing the context.
jokes.explanation: A good payoff — the show has played Michael's supportive-brother speeches straight for three seasons, and now Lindsay weaponizes them as evidence of attraction. The 40-year timeline is also the wrong number (they're twins, not lifetime companions in that sense), adding another layer of confusion. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: I'm just not that into older women.
jokes.setup: Lindsay is trying to seduce Michael; he needs to reject her.
jokes.punchline: Instead of 'you're my sister' (or 'this is insane'), Michael's chosen reason for rejection is her age — three years older — delivered with complete seriousness.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's finest lines. In an absolutely insane situation (his apparent-sister trying to seduce him), Michael chooses the most mundane possible rejection reason — a slight age difference — rather than the obvious 'you're my sister.' The joke works because it suggests Michael's principal objection is her being slightly older, not the incest. Perfect economy, maximum absurdism. High quotability — the line works completely out of context.

jokes.text: You can't do that. / They're my shares. / No, I mean, you can't spoon me like that.
jokes.setup: Michael wakes to find Tobias in bed with him and says 'you can't do that.'
jokes.punchline: Tobias assumes Michael means he can't sell his shares — revealing Tobias was thinking about the business; Michael's actual objection is being spooned.
jokes.explanation: Textbook AD misdirection at its finest. The setup implies business talk; the punchline reveals physical intimacy; and the reversal also characterizes both men perfectly — Tobias is thinking about shares while spooning Michael, and Michael's primary objection is the spooning itself (not the strangeness of being in bed with his brother-in-law). The triple-layer structure is exceptional. Economy is near-perfect. Among the episode's best moments.

jokes.text: We need a new venue for the shareholders' party, and it cannot be a boat. / Now, if you can find something, maybe I can fudge the books. Get you a Bluth Company salary. Maybe call you an events coordinator.
jokes.setup: Michael asks Tobias to find a party venue.
jokes.punchline: Michael's offer of legitimate employment involves 'fudging the books' — he's becoming his father, institutionalizing corruption even for innocent hires.
jokes.explanation: A good character observation — Michael's gradual moral compromise is the episode's theme, and this beat captures it. Loses points for not being particularly funny on its own terms; it's more thematic than comedic.

jokes.text: Can we just lay here for a second while I think about it?
jokes.setup: Michael has offered Tobias a job if he finds a party venue.
jokes.punchline: Tobias asks to 'lay here for a second' — still spooning Michael — framing the strange bed situation as comfortable and desirable from his perspective.
jokes.explanation: Tobias's complete comfort with spooning Michael is one of his running character threads (oblivious homoerotic behavior). The line is a perfect coda to the spoon joke — he wants to stay. Character integration is near-perfect: only Tobias would ask this.

jokes.text: He also mentioned that we all only had three more weeks on Earth and that fossils were just something the Jews buried in 1924.
jokes.setup: Ann's Uncle Paul tells George Michael that Ann has moved in with her boyfriend.
jokes.punchline: The narrator casually tags on that Paul also told George Michael about an imminent apocalypse and an insane conspiracy theory about fossils — delivered with the same weight as the Ann news.
jokes.explanation: A brilliant piece of absurdist narrator comedy. The casual equating of 'Ann moved in with her boyfriend' with '3 weeks until the end of the world' and an antisemitic fossil conspiracy theory — all in one breath — is darkly hilarious. The specificity of '1924' makes the conspiracy funnier. High originality; this kind of narrator absurdism is AD at its best.

jokes.text: And so, feeling that he couldn't go home, George Michael decided to seek out his own creepy uncle.
jokes.setup: George Michael has received bad news from Ann's Uncle Paul.
jokes.punchline: The narrator calls Tobias a 'creepy uncle' — a descriptor that is objectively accurate but would never be said aloud in the show. 'His own creepy uncle' implies all uncles in this world are creepy, and George Michael is specifically selecting his.
jokes.explanation: The narrator's flat diagnosis of Tobias as 'creepy' — said without judgment, as pure fact — is funny. The 'his own' implies the inventory of creepy uncles George Michael could choose from. Solid but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Look, she was better off with us. He wanted to call her Nellie.
jokes.setup: Michael confronts Lucille about adopting Lindsay to spite a competitor.
jokes.punchline: Lucille's primary justification for the adoption is that the biological father wanted to name her 'Nellie' — as if the name is worse than being adopted by the Bluths.
jokes.explanation: Lucille's logic — that avoiding the name 'Nellie' justifies the entire scheme — is perfect Lucille. It's self-serving rationalization dressed up as genuine concern. The callback to 'Nellie from the photo' works for dedicated viewers. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: We loved her just as much as any of you normal kids. More than Gob.
jokes.setup: Lucille claims the family loved Lindsay just as much as their biological children.
jokes.punchline: 'More than Gob' — as a clarification, casually implying Gob is loved least. The qualifier makes the 'reassurance' into an insult for Gob.
jokes.explanation: Three words that are devastatingly funny. 'More than Gob' is the perfect button — it's meant to be reassuring but immediately establishes a family hierarchy in which Gob is the official least-loved child. Lucille says it without hesitation, as if this is common knowledge. Near-perfect economy and character integration. One of the episode's most quotable moments.

jokes.text: We'll pay them off. We'll call it an accrued cash dividend. But they won't get their money until they sign a document at the party promising not to sell for 10 years. / So it's embezzlement, bribery and conspiracy? / And a whole lot of love. / All right, and perjury.
jokes.setup: Lucille proposes a scheme to keep shareholders from selling.
jokes.punchline: Michael catalogues the crimes ('embezzlement, bribery and conspiracy'); Lucille counters with 'and a whole lot of love'; Michael adds 'and perjury' — the legal precision followed by sentimentality followed by more legal precision is the escalating joke.
jokes.explanation: A terrific three-beat escalation. Michael's clinical legal inventory is funny, Lucille's 'whole lot of love' is the absurd non-sequitur, and Michael mechanically adding 'and perjury' treats Lucille's sentimentality as just another item on the list. The rhythm is excellent — the joke is in the structure as much as the content.

jokes.text: We're having it at the Queen Mary. / Queen Mary? That's a ship. / Oh, no, no, no. It's a chain of popular nightclubs. / Although I haven't been to this particular location. / It's on the Long Beach Harbor. / That's the ship. / Don't worry about it. They've actually welded it to the dock.
jokes.setup: Tobias announces the party venue.
jokes.punchline: The escalating revelation that Tobias booked an actual historic ship (the Queen Mary) while believing it was a nightclub chain, culminating in 'They've actually welded it to the dock' as if this is reassuring.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully structured escalation. Each beat raises the stakes: it's a ship → he didn't check → it's in a harbor → that's the ship → welded to the dock (as reassurance). 'Welded to the dock' is the payoff — Tobias's attempt to reassure everyone backfires because a docked ship is still a ship. The structure is impeccable and each new revelation makes Tobias's incompetence more impressive.

jokes.text: Don't worry about it. They've actually welded it to the dock.
jokes.setup: Michael objects that the Queen Mary is a ship (Buster's biggest fear after seals).
jokes.punchline: Tobias reassures them it's 'welded to the dock' — as if that makes it not a ship, and as if welding resolves Buster's ocean trauma.
jokes.explanation: Tobias's logic — 'it's welded to the dock, so it's fine' — is absurdist character comedy. The welding detail is specific and weird enough to be funny while also being a plausible real-world solution that completely misses the point. Good economy and character work.

jokes.text: Although I should call the Hot Cops and tell them to come up with something more nautically themed. / Hot Sailors. / Better yet, Hot Se... / I like Hot Sailors. / Mm, me too.
jokes.setup: Tobias is planning entertainment for the party and mentions the Hot Cops.
jokes.punchline: Tobias starts to say 'Hot Seamen' (a nautical pun on 'semen'), gets cut off, and Michael and Lucille both enthusiastically agree on 'Hot Sailors' — the shared enthusiasm for the male strippers is the joke, with Tobias's near-miss double entendre as the setup.
jokes.explanation: The near-miss 'Hot Se...' is a classic AD near-obscenity that the audience completes themselves. The double joke is that Tobias is cut off AND that Michael and Lucille's enthusiastic 'Mm, me too' about male strippers is its own character beat — the Bluths' casual comfort with this is funny. Running gag payoff with good new wrinkles.

jokes.text: George Michael was about to come across a hot seaman of his own.
jokes.setup: The party is being planned with 'Hot Sailors.'
jokes.punchline: The narrator's transition to George Michael uses 'hot seaman' — completing the double-entendre Tobias couldn't finish, while ostensibly referring to Gob (a seaman/sailor on his boat).
jokes.explanation: The narrator completes Tobias's aborted punchline as a scene transition — elegant and precise. The double meaning (Gob as an actual seaman, plus the 'hot seaman' from the stripper conversation) is perfectly deployed. The structure of using a transition line as a callback payoff is quintessential AD. Strong economy — the line does triple duty.

jokes.text: Who? / Ann. / Her?
jokes.setup: George Michael learns Gob has been dating 'his girlfriend.'
jokes.punchline: 'Her?' — the series-long running gag about Ann being unremarkable, deployed by Gob in his most dismissive register.
jokes.explanation: One word. Maximum impact for fans of the series. 'Her?' from Gob, who is literally dating Ann, is the perfection of the running gag — the most dismissive person about Ann IS dating her. Economy is perfect (10/10 — you cannot be more economical than a single word). Lower originality because it's the established gag, but the execution and context make it land harder than ever.

jokes.text: Gob had been dating Ann since he awarded her third place in an inner beauty pageant.
jokes.setup: The narrator explains how Gob and Ann started dating.
jokes.punchline: An 'inner beauty pageant' where Ann won THIRD PLACE — Gob ran a pageant judging inner beauty (an oxymoron for a pageant) and Ann didn't even win, yet here they are.
jokes.explanation: A dense, layered absurdist gag. 'Inner beauty pageant' is already funny (it's an oxymoron — you can't judge inner beauty in a pageant format). Ann winning THIRD PLACE at an inner beauty pageant implies either there's a lot of inner beauty competition in their community OR Gob's standards are truly incomprehensible. Then he dates the third-place finisher. Perfect Gob absurdism.

jokes.text: What? I thought that's what all you guys lived for. / While Gob debased everything they believed in.
jokes.setup: Ann and her religious friends try to teach Gob about the Holy Trinity.
jokes.punchline: Gob's response ('I thought that's what all you guys lived for') implies the Holy Trinity refers to something sexual — and the narrator confirms he debased everything they believed in.
jokes.explanation: Gob's instinct to sexualize religious terminology is very on-brand, and the narrator's flat 'Gob debased everything they believed in' is the perfect summary. The joke is efficient and character-specific.

jokes.text: It's really not that big of a deal, George Michael. I'm 18 now. And besides, we really haven't even done anything. Practically nothing. But what are you gonna do? The girl obviously needs a man. And that's when George Michael became one.
jokes.setup: Gob dismisses George Michael's concern about Ann.
jokes.punchline: The narrator's 'And that's when George Michael became one' — implying George Michael punched Gob — is the payoff, turning Gob's condescending 'she needs a man' into his own humiliation.
jokes.explanation: The narrator's payoff is precisely timed and multi-layered: 'the girl needs a man' → George Michael IS that man → he proves it by punching Gob. The phrase 'became one' works on two levels (became a man/became the man Ann needed). The revelation of the punch comes much later (17:30), which makes this a plant for that payoff. Excellent structure.

jokes.text: Do you think there's any chance this ship could break away from the dock? / We're on a ship?
jokes.setup: George Sr. asks if the ship could break free — clearly hoping to escape.
jokes.punchline: Buster has somehow not realized they're on a ship — despite his severe ocean/boat phobia.
jokes.explanation: Buster's obliviousness despite his extreme phobia is perfect character comedy. The joke is that his anxiety is so generalized he didn't notice the specific trigger. 'We're on a ship?' lands well because of how much the episode has established his terror.

jokes.text: Maeby trying to get people to sign her TV release forms by calling it a petition to make TV better.
jokes.setup: Maeby needs family members to sign releases for a TV show about their lives.
jokes.punchline: She disguises the release forms as a 'petition to make TV better' — a perfectly ironic scheme, since the Bluth family story IS the TV show (meta-reference to Arrested Development itself).
jokes.explanation: The meta-joke that the Bluth family story IS the TV show being signed away is very AD. Maeby's scheme is appropriately mercenary and character-consistent. Good rewatch value for the meta-layer.

jokes.text: Oh, I kind of like Skating with Celebrities.
jokes.setup: Maeby argues TV needs improvement to get Buster's signature.
jokes.punchline: Buster's defense of 'Skating with Celebrities' — a notoriously low-quality reality show — as counter-evidence to TV needing improvement.
jokes.explanation: A topical joke about a real show — dates badly and isn't particularly inventive, but Buster's genuine enthusiasm for low-quality TV is on-brand. Functional but weak.

jokes.text: Sign here that you won't sell. / Oh, I don't need to sell, Lucille, because now I know that we're not related, I'm going to marry Michael. / I always saw him with a younger girl.
jokes.setup: Lucille approaches Lindsay to get her signature; Lindsay announces she plans to marry Michael.
jokes.punchline: Lucille's response — 'I always saw him with a younger girl' — is devastatingly on-brand, simultaneously insulting Lindsay's age and revealing that she's thought about Michael's romantic prospects.
jokes.explanation: Lucille's 'I always saw him with a younger girl' is a masterpiece of casual cruelty — it's both an insult to Lindsay AND an inadvertent alignment with Michael's own rejection reason. The economy is excellent; the character integration is near-perfect. Lucille doesn't even react to the incest proposal, only to the age implication.

jokes.text: Gob, angry at Michael for sending his son to the docks, went into a kind of knee-jerk revenge mode. / Why go for the best when you can go for the rest... of your life with a younger man?
jokes.setup: Gob wants revenge on Michael and approaches a woman (Kitty, revealed next).
jokes.punchline: 'Why go for the best when you can go for the rest of your life with a younger man?' — the sentence pivots mid-phrase from what sounds like a seduction line into a proposal/commitment statement, revealing Gob's revenge involves seducing someone to spite Michael.
jokes.explanation: A clever structural trick — the sentence is written to misdirect, breaking mid-thought to pivot meaning. 'The rest of your life with a younger man' subverts the cliché seduction line into something both romantic and self-aggrandizing. The structure is the joke. Gob's chaotic revenge logic is perfectly captured.

jokes.text: Kitty likes to scratch. / Oh, God! Mom! Mom!
jokes.setup: Gob is about to have a revenge-fueled encounter with Kitty.
jokes.punchline: Gob's horrified 'Oh, God! Mom! Mom!' implies something disturbing is happening — Kitty's enthusiasm is so alarming that Gob is calling for his mother.
jokes.explanation: Kitty's announcement 'Kitty likes to scratch' is already funny as pure character bravado, but Gob calling for his mother during the encounter is the perfect punchline — his bravado immediately collapses. Character-specific and well-timed.

jokes.text: Well, that was a freebie.
jokes.setup: Maeby discovers that Lucille's bribe (promising $100,000 if people sign) can be used to get signatures on her own release forms.
jokes.punchline: 'That was a freebie' — Maeby's mercenary casualness about benefiting from her grandmother's bribery scheme.
jokes.explanation: Economy is the joke's strength — three words that perfectly capture Maeby's amoral opportunism. Character integration is strong. Loses points for being a brief button rather than a fully developed joke.

jokes.text: My brother Gob, who I know would never do anything to hurt anyone in this family. / Except for dating his nephew's girlfriend and hitting on his adopted sister.
jokes.setup: Michael gives a sincere speech about his family members.
jokes.punchline: The narrator immediately footnotes each compliment with the accurate, damning exception — 'Except for dating his nephew's girlfriend and hitting on his adopted sister.'
jokes.explanation: The narrator-as-fact-checker during Michael's sincere speech is a classic AD structural move. The specific exceptions ('dating his nephew's girlfriend AND hitting on his adopted sister') arrive as a compound indictment that lands hard. The economy is excellent — packing two major Gob crimes into a single parenthetical.

jokes.text: Buster, who faced his biggest fear to be here today. / Second biggest.
jokes.setup: Michael credits Buster for facing his fear (the ocean) to attend.
jokes.punchline: Buster quietly corrects him — the ocean is his SECOND biggest fear. This plants the payoff for his actual biggest fear appearing moments later.
jokes.explanation: A crucial structural beat — the second 'second biggest fear' reference makes clear we're being set up for a payoff. Economy is exceptional (two words: 'Second biggest'). The rewatch bonus is very high because on second viewing you know exactly what's coming. The structural foreshadowing is one of AD's signature techniques.

jokes.text: Buster, who faced his biggest fear to be here today. / Second biggest.
jokes.setup: Michael credits Buster for facing 'his biggest fear' to attend the ship party.
jokes.punchline: Buster corrects: the ship is only his 'second biggest' fear — the first being the seal that ate his hand.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully timed callback. Buster's correction mid-speech is perfect comedy timing, and 'second biggest' implies there's a fear hierarchy we've come to know. The economy of the correction is outstanding. Rewatch bonus for how it tracks across the episode.

jokes.text: And my sister, Lindsay. Yes, she is my sister, no matter what any piece of paper says.
jokes.setup: Michael gives a sincere speech affirming family bonds.
jokes.punchline: Michael asserting Lindsay is his sister 'no matter what any piece of paper says' — he's only just learned she's adopted, and his sincerity is genuine, but the audience knows he recently rejected her romantic advance using the three-year age difference as his excuse rather than sisterhood.
jokes.explanation: The joke is the gap between Michael's sincere sentiment and the audience's knowledge that his actual reason for rejecting Lindsay was her being three years older, not sisterhood. It's situational irony that rewards the attentive viewer.

jokes.text: Lindsay and her husband, Tobias... deep down, they both love each other very, very much.
jokes.setup: Michael gives a family toast.
jokes.punchline: The 'deep down' qualifier is doing enormous work — the audience knows Tobias and Lindsay's marriage is a disaster of mutual indifference and Tobias's closeted homosexuality. 'Very, very much' overcorrects into obvious insincerity.
jokes.explanation: The joke is in Michael's verbal hedge ('deep down') applied to an obviously broken marriage, with the 'very, very much' being the tell that he doesn't believe it either. Solid irony, but it's a familiar beat in the Tobias/Lindsay dynamic.

jokes.text: It wasn't exactly a turn-on.
jokes.setup: Michael cries during his speech. The narrator observes the room's reaction.
jokes.punchline: The narrator's dry 'It wasn't exactly a turn-on' implies that this was specifically problematic for someone who WAS turned on by Michael — almost certainly Lindsay.
jokes.explanation: The economy is exceptional — five words that imply someone in the room was previously attracted to the crying man. The callback to Lindsay's earlier advance makes this land harder for attentive viewers. Clean, dry, perfectly placed.

jokes.text: Whoo !@%$# baby. / Looks like George Michael got his mom back today, huh?
jokes.setup: Gob observes Michael crying during his speech.
jokes.punchline: Gob's comment that George Michael 'got his mom back' by seeing his father cry is simultaneously an insult to Michael's masculinity and a genuine observation about Michael's maternal qualities — and 'Whoo !@%$# baby' implies Gob found the crying weirdly exciting.
jokes.explanation: Gob's characteristic cruelty toward Michael is on-brand, and 'got his mom back' is a specific insult that lands. The 'Whoo !@%$# baby' adds absurdist flavor. Decent but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Yeah, well, where is George Michael, by the way? / He came by the yacht. Gave me this shiner. / He hit you? What for? / You know teenagers.
jokes.setup: Michael asks about George Michael. Gob reveals George Michael punched him and gave him a black eye.
jokes.punchline: Gob's 'You know teenagers' — as if a nephew punching his uncle on a yacht over a girlfriend is typical teenage behavior — is perfectly understated.
jokes.explanation: 'You know teenagers' as an explanation for being punched by your nephew on a yacht over your relationship with his ex-girlfriend is a perfect deadpan understatement. Gob treats the situation as boringly routine. Character integration is high — only Gob would normalize this. Justifies 8+ for economy and delivery.

jokes.text: He came by the yacht. Gave me this shiner. / He hit you? What for? / You know teenagers. Probably lashing out at you because of who I'm dating. / Who are you dating? / Ann. / Her?
jokes.setup: Gob reveals George Michael punched him and gave him a black eye.
jokes.punchline: The reveal that George Michael punched Gob AND the 'Her?' payoff again — Michael's instinct is still to dismiss Ann even when learning his son punched someone over her.
jokes.explanation: The double payoff — George Michael actually punching Gob AND the 'Her?' gag — is satisfying. The 'Her?' here is even better than the earlier one because Michael is finding out about a violent act motivated by Ann, and he still can't get past 'her?' The callback structure is tight.

jokes.text: Don't worry. I didn't fight back or anything. I was like, 'Oh, no, take the yacht. Please don't hurt me.' Just build up the little guy's self-esteem.
jokes.setup: Gob explains what happened when George Michael hit him.
jokes.punchline: Gob claims he let George Michael win and gave up his yacht as a kind gesture to 'build up the little guy's self-esteem' — a transparently self-serving retroactive framing of being legitimately beaten by a teenager.
jokes.explanation: Classic Gob self-deception — reframing humiliation as generosity. 'Just build up the little guy's self-esteem' is pure Gob: he's covering his embarrassment with magnanimity while making it about himself. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Never seen a CEO cry like that before, except at his sentencing.
jokes.setup: Michael has just cried at his own CEO announcement party in front of investors.
jokes.punchline: An investor notes the only other time they've seen a CEO cry like that is at sentencing — darkly implying that sentencing is the natural endpoint for Bluth Company leadership.
jokes.explanation: A sharp, well-structured observation that works on multiple levels: (1) investor horror at Michael's crying, (2) the casual familiarity with CEO sentencing (implying this happens often in their world), and (3) foreshadowing that the Bluths will eventually face their day in court. The economy is excellent — 14 words doing heavy lifting. High rewatch value.

jokes.text: I've made a huge mistake.
jokes.setup: Michael realizes he's sold out his values to hold the company together.
jokes.punchline: The line 'I've made a huge mistake' is Gob's signature catchphrase used by Michael — typically said by Gob about something trivial; here used by Michael about something genuinely serious, which inverts the gag.
jokes.explanation: The most meta-textually rich joke in the episode. Michael using Gob's catchphrase at the moment of his genuine moral reckoning is layered: (1) it's the payoff of a running gag, (2) it's actually appropriate here (unlike when Gob says it), (3) it suggests Michael is becoming more like his family by the end of the series. Perfect character integration (Michael + Gob's DNA), perfect economy. One of the episode's strongest moments.

jokes.text: That's who you're putting in charge? / I've made a huge mistake.
jokes.setup: An investor questions Michael's fitness after seeing him cry.
jokes.punchline: 'I've made a huge mistake' — an AD running gag, typically Gob's line, is now delivered by Michael. It's a phrase that signals an AD character's realization that they've done something catastrophically wrong.
jokes.explanation: One of AD's most iconic catchphrases, deployed at the series' climax for maximum resonance. The quotability is off the charts — it's arguably the show's most famous line. Impact score reflects the cultural weight rather than just the in-episode joke.

jokes.text: Will you take the company for 20% above market? / Ten, and I'll take the company off your hands right now. / Fifteen and I'll throw in Nellie. / She's 40. / Twelve.
jokes.setup: Michael is trying to sell the company under duress; Sitwell negotiates.
jokes.punchline: Michael throws in 'Nellie' (Lindsay) as a sweetener in a business negotiation; Sitwell counters with her age ('She's 40') as a devaluing factor, and they settle lower. Lindsay has been commodified in a business transaction.
jokes.explanation: A stunning joke that combines multiple episode threads: the adoption (Nellie), the age obsession (She's 40), and the Bluths' casual objectification of family members as business assets. 'She's 40' as a counter-offer in a corporate negotiation is both darkly hilarious and perfectly structured. Sitwell counter-offering LOWER because of her age is the devastating punchline. One of the episode's top moments.

jokes.text: Are those police boats? / Oh, my God, the SEC. / They still have boats?
jokes.setup: The SEC arrives by water to board the Queen Mary.
jokes.punchline: Gob's question — 'They still have boats?' — treats the existence of government watercraft as surprising and possibly outdated information, revealing his complete detachment from reality.
jokes.explanation: Gob's question is absurdist but perfectly in character. The SEC is boarding a ship — of course they have boats — but Gob processes this as news. 'They still have boats?' implies he thought the SEC's nautical program was discontinued. Economy is excellent.

jokes.text: I can't help but enjoy the irony that you might be losing three years of your life.
jokes.setup: The SEC is boarding; George Sr. might face prison time.
jokes.punchline: Michael notes the irony that George Sr. — who caused Michael to 'lose' three years of his life managing the family — might now lose three years of his life to prison.
jokes.explanation: The callback to the show's premise (Michael's three sacrificed years) is well-executed. Michael's satisfaction in the irony is both darkly funny and emotionally resonant. Good structural callback to the episode's opening.

jokes.text: George invited me. Oh, stupid. / Oscar, when are you going to learn there's no such thing as free shrimp?
jokes.setup: Oscar appears on the yacht believing George invited him.
jokes.punchline: Michael's response — 'there's no such thing as free shrimp' — is a riff on 'no such thing as a free lunch,' applied to Oscar's terminal naivety about his twin brother's motives. Oscar fell for a shrimp invitation as a setup.
jokes.explanation: The idiom substitution ('free shrimp' for 'free lunch') is funny because shrimp is such a specific, food-party-related thing. Oscar's self-correction ('Oh, stupid') adds pathos. Michael's line is a good character observation — Oscar eternally falls for George's traps.

jokes.text: Or someone who used to be. / Annyong? / Annyong. / Annyong? / Annyong. But my real name is... / Hello. / Hello? / Annyong.
jokes.setup: The SEC informant is revealed to be Annyong (the Korean boy adopted by the Bluths), whose name means 'hello' — and his real name is also 'Hello.'
jokes.punchline: The confusion between 'Annyong' (hello in Korean), his actual name 'Hello,' and the conversational 'hello' creates a perfect loop of miscommunication that is both a running gag payoff and a new joke.
jokes.explanation: This is a series-long payoff that simultaneously resolves the Annyong running gag AND creates a new layer of wordplay. Annyong (Korean for hello) reveals his real name is 'Hello' in English — so the Bluths were inadvertently calling him by his name all along without knowing it. The exchange that follows ('Hello? / Annyong') is a perfect comedic loop. The structure is exceptional — three seasons of setup for a punchline that works on linguistic, character, and narrative levels simultaneously. Tied for the episode's top craft moment.

jokes.text: Because my grandfather vowed one day he would get even for banana stand you stole from him.
jokes.setup: Annyong explains why he informed on the Bluths.
jokes.punchline: The entire Annyong storyline — three seasons of a seemingly random adoption — was motivated by his grandfather's deportation and stolen banana stand concept, tying back to the show's most iconic prop.
jokes.explanation: This is the show's most elaborate callback payoff — the banana stand (series icon, 'there's always money in the banana stand') originated with a Korean immigrant whom Lucille had deported, and Annyong's adoption was his grandson's decades-long revenge scheme. Three seasons of Annyong barely speaking, doing nothing, being background furniture — all of it was setup. The craft score of 9.0 is justified because this requires five seasons of planning in the writers' room and delivers a laugh that rewards every season of attention. Arguably the episode's most impressive structural achievement.

jokes.text: Hey, Maeby ever mention my name?
jokes.setup: In the middle of a federal boarding action, Gob asks Annyong if Maeby ever mentions him.
jokes.punchline: Gob's complete inability to prioritize — asking about his reputation with a 15-year-old during an SEC raid — is perfectly oblivious.
jokes.explanation: Pure Gob character comedy — his vanity and social insecurity override any situational awareness. The timing (mid-raid) makes it funnier. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: But Lucille was not going peacefully. / You boys know how to shovel coal? / I don't even wanna tell you what these guys thought that meant.
jokes.setup: Lucille takes control of the Queen Mary to flee; she needs help with the engine.
jokes.punchline: The narrator refuses to describe what the Hot Sailors interpreted 'shovel coal' to mean — implying their interpretation was extremely sexual, while Lucille literally meant shovel coal to power the steam engine.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed innuendo joke that uses narrative withholding ('I don't even wanna tell you') as the punchline. The Hot Sailors are strippers who interpreted a coal-shoveling request sexually — and the narrator's refusal to describe it lets the audience's imagination do the work. The use of the narrator as an unreliable censor is excellent craft. Strong character integration for Lucille (oblivious to the strippers' interpretation while issuing commands).

jokes.text: Buddy, Maeby is not your cousin. / What? / Yeah, I found out a couple days ago. Aunt Lindsay was adopted. / Why didn't you tell me this? / Because you can't be with Maeby. You're 15.
jokes.setup: George Michael has fled because he can't be around Maeby as his cousin.
jokes.punchline: Michael reveals Maeby isn't actually his cousin — then immediately explains why he withheld this information: because George Michael is 15. The revelation and suppression in the same breath.
jokes.explanation: The reversal is well-structured — Michael gives George Michael exactly what he wanted (she's not your cousin!) and then immediately takes it away (you're 15). The cruelty of withholding this information for 'a couple days' while George Michael suffered is a good character beat. Strong structure.

jokes.text: She might not be a blood relative, but she is still family, and that's a bond that lasts forever. You don't wanna rock that boat.
jokes.setup: Michael counsels George Michael to leave Maeby alone despite not being blood relatives.
jokes.punchline: 'You don't wanna rock that boat' — said while literally on a boat that is about to be rocked (Lucille is about to commandeer it and sail away).
jokes.explanation: A perfectly placed idiom that immediately becomes literal — the boat IS about to rock. The double meaning of 'rock the boat' (disrupt family harmony / physically move the vessel) is sharp, and the timing of Lucille taking control right after makes it a structural callback payoff. Good AD density.

jokes.text: What the hell was that? / It was his mother, who moments earlier had taken control of another queen.
jokes.setup: The boat suddenly lurches. Michael is confused.
jokes.punchline: The narrator explains Lucille took control of 'another queen' — the Queen Mary (the ship) — but 'queen' also refers to Lucille herself (she's described as 'the queen' earlier by George Sr.).
jokes.explanation: The 'another queen' wordplay is satisfying — one queen controlling another queen. The callback to George Sr.'s chess metaphor adds a layer for attentive viewers. Good economy and structure.

jokes.text: It's moving! Oh, my God! / And Buster faced his second biggest fear. / I can't swim! I can't swim! / And then his first.
jokes.setup: The ship breaks free from the dock. Buster is on board, having already established both his second biggest fear (ocean) and an unnamed first biggest fear.
jokes.punchline: The narrator tags 'And then his first' — followed by what is clearly a seal encounter (the seal that previously ate his hand is his first biggest fear).
jokes.explanation: The structural payoff of the 'second biggest fear / first biggest fear' setup is impeccable. Over the course of the episode (and seasons), the show planted 'second biggest' twice, making the audience wonder what could be worse. The answer — the seal, the very animal that ate his hand, showing up in the ocean — is both logical and perfectly absurd. 'And then his first' (four words, maximum impact) is a masterclass in comedic economy. One of the great AD structural jokes. Craft score of 9.0 is justified.

jokes.text: It's moving! Oh, my God!
jokes.setup: Lucille has commandeered the ship. The Queen Mary — supposedly 'welded to the dock' — is now moving.
jokes.punchline: Buster's horror at the ship moving is the payoff to (a) Tobias's 'it's welded to the dock' reassurance and (b) Buster's ocean/ship phobia.
jokes.explanation: The payoff to 'it's welded to the dock' is satisfying. Buster's reaction is the punchline to Tobias's earlier false reassurance. The callback structure is clean.

jokes.text: Oh, come on!
jokes.setup: Buster's first biggest fear (presumably the seal) arrives just as he's already dealing with his second biggest fear (the ocean).
jokes.punchline: Buster's resigned 'Oh, come on!' — the most understated possible reaction to being confronted by the animal that ate his hand while drowning in the ocean.
jokes.explanation: Three syllables of perfectly calibrated character response. Buster's resigned 'Oh, come on!' in the face of cosmic misfortune is exactly right — it's not screaming or despair, it's the exasperation of someone for whom disaster is a lifestyle. Maximum economy (10/10). The joke is inseparable from Buster's character — only Buster says 'Oh, come on!' to a seal attack.

jokes.text: And the two grand ladies made their escape together.
jokes.setup: Lucille is escaping on the Queen Mary.
jokes.punchline: 'Two grand ladies' — Lucille and the Queen Mary are both 'grand ladies,' but 'grand' also refers to their both being associated with enormous money and status. The narrator treats their escape as a dignified pairing.
jokes.explanation: A nice piece of narrator wordplay. 'Two grand ladies' is both a literal description and a character judgment. The dignity of the phrasing amid the chaos of an SEC raid and seal attack is perfectly AD. Elegant but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Lucille Bluth, please pull over and step away from the historic ship.
jokes.setup: Law enforcement pursues Lucille on the escaping Queen Mary.
jokes.punchline: 'Please pull over and step away from the historic ship' — traffic stop language applied to a massive ocean liner is absurdly inappropriate and hilarious.
jokes.explanation: The incongruity between car-stop language ('pull over,' 'step away') and the context of a historic ocean liner is the joke. 'Historic ship' added at the end implies the officer's primary concern is property damage, not law enforcement. Good absurdist structure.

jokes.text: Gangy's trying to make a break for it. / I guess we should go back. Family sticks together, huh? / Yeah, on the other hand, we do have a full tank of gas, a house in Cabo, and 500 grand in cashier's checks. What say we give them no choice but to keep themselves all together for a while?
jokes.setup: The family sees Lucille trying to escape; Michael and George Michael consider going back to help.
jokes.punchline: George Michael (or Michael) pivots to 'on the other hand' — revealing they have a getaway plan of their own: gas, Cabo house, and $500,000 in cashier's checks. They're going to escape TOO, leaving the family to fend for itself.
jokes.explanation: The series finale's central irony — Michael, who spent three seasons refusing to abandon his family, finally abandons them — is delivered as a comedic beat rather than a dramatic one. 'On the other hand' is the pivot, and the list (gas, Cabo, cashier's checks) is perfectly structured escalation. The callback to George Sr.'s Cabo hideout, the echo of the Bluth family's own escape behavior, and Michael finally becoming what he fought against are all present. One of the episode's most important and best-constructed jokes. Highly earned.

jokes.text: Yeah, on the other hand... we do have a full tank of gas, a house in Cabo, and 500 grand in cashier's checks.
jokes.setup: Michael has been arguing all episode for family loyalty and staying together.
jokes.punchline: Michael immediately starts calculating his escape — gas, Cabo, $500k in cashier's checks. He's become exactly the family member he criticized all episode.
jokes.explanation: A beautiful thematic payoff. Michael, who spent the entire episode trying to keep the family together and criticizing others for selling out, now itemizes his own escape plan using the exact same elements (Cabo, money) he criticized his father for. The callback to George Sr.'s Cabo hideout is perfect. This is the series finale's thematic punchline.

jokes.text: What say we give them no choice but to keep themselves all together for a while?
jokes.setup: Michael is leaving the family behind as the SEC closes in.
jokes.punchline: Michael's last line mirrors the show's premise ('keep them all together') but now he's the one escaping — and his leaving will force the family to deal with their own consequences. The irony inverts the entire series' dynamic.
jokes.explanation: The series finale's ultimate punchline — a direct callback to the show's premise ('the one son who had no choice but to keep them all together'), now inverted. Michael leaves and frames it as giving the family 'no choice but to keep themselves together.' Every word is doing double duty. This is the kind of final joke that earns its place in TV history — the entire series is the setup. Craft total of 9.1 is the episode's highest. The earned vs. cheap score is 9.5 because three seasons of setup make this the most earned laugh in the episode.

jokes.text: It was Arrested Development.
jokes.setup: The show ends with the narrator's typical sign-off.
jokes.punchline: The use of past tense ('It was') — not 'This is' — marks the show's ending. The show is speaking its own eulogy.
jokes.explanation: Three words. Past tense. The show's title used as its tombstone. The shift from 'It's' (00:34) to 'It was' is the most economical series finale joke possible — acknowledging the cancellation while maintaining the narrator's dry remove. This is what 9.0 structure looks like: perfect form serving perfect function. Economy is 10/10.

jokes.text: Michael wakes up to another strange bedfellow after his first night at sea.
jokes.setup: Epilogue begins; Michael has escaped on a boat.
jokes.punchline: A 'strange bedfellow' — calling back to Tobias spooning him earlier, implying Michael has now attracted another strange co-sleeper on his escape voyage.
jokes.explanation: A solid epilogue callback — 'strange bedfellow' echoes the Tobias spooning situation and continues the joke that Michael can't even escape without someone in his bed. Functional but not exceptional in an episode full of stronger callbacks.

jokes.text: No, I, uh... I don't see it as a series. Maybe a movie.
jokes.setup: Maeby pitches the Bluth family story as a TV series to a Hollywood icon.
jokes.punchline: The Hollywood icon (implied to be Ron Howard, the show's narrator and executive producer) says he doesn't see it as a series — 'Maybe a movie' — a direct meta-joke about AD's own cancellation and the long-rumored AD movie.
jokes.explanation: The episode's single most meta and most brilliant joke. Ron Howard (the narrator/EP) appears in the AD universe and rejects a TV pitch about the Bluths' story — the exact show he's been narrating — in favor of 'maybe a movie.' This is a direct fourth-wall moment about the show's cancellation and the AD movie that had been discussed. The craft score of 9.3 is justified: it's the culmination of the entire episode's meta-thread, executed with perfect economy, and it's the final word of the series. Character integration is 9.5 because Ron Howard's real-world role makes this uniquely possible on this show and no other.

tmdb_overview: After returning home from Iraq, George Sr. is finally cleared of all charges, and Michael is relieved to be the head of a successful company for a change. The real brains behind the madness is revealed, and just as the family begins to celebrate their victory, a whole new set of problems emerge.
insight: Season 3 finale delivers 60 jokes in 60 minutes—the show's most balanced density before the creative collapse.
